perturbation.original_text: (6) The payment of the Advertising Cost Compensation shall occur monthly insofar as the sum due exceeds a value of 100 EUR / 100 USD. If this is not the case, the sum due can be retained until a total value of at least 100 EUR / 100 USD is reached, at the latest, upon termination of the Affiliate's participation in the Program.
perturbation.changed_text: (6) The payment of the Advertising Cost Compensation shall occur quarterly insofar as the sum due exceeds a value of 500 EUR / 500 USD. If this is not the case, the sum due can be retained indefinitely, even after termination of the Affiliate's participation in the Program, at the discretion of element 5.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment frequency to quarterly and increasing the minimum payment amount, while also allowing indefinite retention of funds even after termination, introduces a conflict with wage payment laws. This is because many jurisdictions require more frequent payments and prohibit indefinite withholding of earned compensation.
perturbation.law_explanation: This section mandates the payment of minimum wage and overtime compensation to employees. By delaying or indefinitely withholding earned commissions, this revised contract violates the spirit and potentially the letter of wage payment laws by not ensuring timely and predictable compensation.
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters payment terms in a way that contradicts potential wage payment laws, which typically mandate timely and predictable compensation. While Snippet 1 is incomplete, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both suggest that delaying payments indefinitely, especially after termination, could violate wage payment laws. The change from monthly to quarterly payments, coupled with indefinite retention, exacerbates this concern, making the contradiction direct and impactful.

perturbation.original_text: WHITHIN 14 DAYS OF THE ANNOUNCEMENT OF CHANGED PARTICIPATION TERMS, AFFILIATE AND SOFTWARE PUBLISHER MAY OBJECT TO SUCH CHANGES. IF NO OBJECTION IS RECEIVED DURING THIS TIME, THE PARTICIPANT IS DEEMED TO HAVE ACCEPTED THE CHANGES AND THEY WILL BECOME PART OF THE EXISTING RELATIONS UNDER THE AGREEMENT.
perturbation.changed_text: AFFILIATE AND SOFTWARE PUBLISHER HAVE 7 DAYS TO OBJECT TO CHANGES. FAILURE TO OBJECT WITHIN 7 DAYS CONSTITUTES ACCEPTANCE, REGARDLESS OF WHETHER THE PARTICIPANT HAS READ THE ANNOUNCEMENT
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the objection period to 7 days and stating agreement is assumed regardless of awareness is an issue. Contract law typically requires reasonable notice and opportunity to review changes. Reducing the time and explicitly disregarding awareness of changes makes the clause potentially unconscionable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-207. Additional Terms in Acceptance or Confirmation
perturbation.law_explanation: This section deals with how additional terms become part of a contract. By shortening the time and ignoring that the announcement may not be read, this modification violates UCC principles requiring fairness and reasonable opportunity to review the contract before accepting modifications.

View more information about the ALI collections The National Bankruptcy Archives (NBA) In 2000, the Biddle Law Library and the American College of Bankruptcy collaborated to establish the National Bankruptcy Archives (NBA), a national repository of materials relating to the history of debtor-creditor relations, bankruptcy, and the reorganization of debt. The NBA currently contains over 1,000 linear feet of personal papers and institutional records. Trent Collection consists of books, periodicals, articles, and audio tapes concerning the history of education and practice of Black lawyers in the United States. The mailing address is: University of Pennsylvania Carey Law School Biddle Law Library - Archives Department 3501 Sansom Street Philadelphia, PA 19104-3406 For questions about the Archives, the Rare Books Collection, or other special collections, please email the Archives Department at biddlearchives@law.upenn.edu . The Archives is open Tuesday-Friday, 10AM to 4PM. Appointments should be made 2 weeks in advance of the visit. Visitors will be asked to show identification. Policies Registration All researchers are asked to fill out our Researcher Registration Form. This form can be filled out prior to your visit or on the day of your arrival. Archives - Researcher Registration Form On-site Research Items in the Archives do not circulate. If you wish to come to the Archives to conduct research, you may do so free of charge. However, you must contact the department ahead of time to schedule an appointment. Appointments should be made 2 weeks in advance of desired research date. Visitors to the Law School will be asked to show identification. Remote Research For patrons who cannot travel to the Law School to conduct research, the Archives will assist in the identification of potentially relevant materials for your research topic or for photocopying. We do not transcribe, paraphrase, or otherwise interpret information for the researcher. Research requests generally take 2-3 weeks to complete, depending on the nature of the question and the number of outstanding requests at the time of submission. In addition, the Archives can only offer one hour of research assistance, patrons are asked to make an appointment for requests that will take longer than one hour to complete. Please also note that remote research requests are limited, and the Archives can only handle copying/scanning requests up to 20 individual documents. If a request exceeds this number, we suggest you schedule an appointment to visit the Archives in person. Handling Materials Wash hands before touching materials. Protective gloves may be required. Remove only one folder from a box at a time. Documents should be maintained in the exact order they are arranged in each file. If a document is out of order, notify the Archives staff. Make no marks or erasures on any document. Use strips provided to mark pages for copying. Eating or drinking is not permitted. Use pencils only, no pens or markers. Laptop
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the objection period to 7 days and states agreement is assumed regardless of awareness, potentially contradicting the UCC principles requiring fairness and reasonable opportunity to review contract modifications. This is identified as an Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction, where a time-sensitive obligation (objection period) is shortened, and awareness is disregarded, potentially violating fairness and reasonable opportunity to review as argued in the law explanation. The scraped snippets, while not directly stating the rule, indirectly highlight the importance of legal archives and the UCC, providing context to the modification's significance.

perturbation.original_text: (8) The invoicing is deemed to be approved if the Affiliate does not object in writing, including a statement of reasons, within four weeks.
perturbation.changed_text: (8) The invoicing is deemed to be approved if the Affiliate does not object in writing, including a statement of reasons, within one week. Any dispute must be settled within element 5 internal process.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the time to dispute an invoice to one week and forcing the affiliate to use only internal company processes directly contradict the affiliate's rights. A business usually must give a reasonable amount of time to detect and solve invoicing errors. Furthermore, mandating only an internal process for resolution restricts legal rights.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Fair Credit Billing Act, 15 U.S. Code § 1666
perturbation.law_explanation: By limiting the timeframe for disputing invoices and mandating only an internal dispute resolution process, this contract term could violate consumer protection laws that ensure fair billing practices and access to legal remedies. By limiting the timeframe for dispute, it also potentially runs afoul of general contract law principles about unconscionable terms.

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the time to dispute an invoice to one week and mandates an internal dispute process. The Perturbation Explanation states that businesses usually must give a reasonable time to detect and solve invoicing errors, and that mandating only an internal process for resolution restricts legal rights. The Law Explanation states that this violates consumer protection laws that ensure fair billing practices and access to legal remedies. Although the provided scraped snippets do not directly address consumer protection laws or fair billing practices, the Perturbation and Law Explanations provide a strong basis to believe that the modified contract introduces an inconsistency creating a legal contradiction.
